metadata.description: SmartPatch Application Controls Catalogue - APP-01 to APP-04
metadata.author: SmartPatch Team
metadata.source: Microsoft Security Baselines, CIS Benchmarks, NIST

controls.name: Disable Windows PowerShell 2.0
controls.description: Remove or disable the legacy PowerShell 2.0 engine to eliminate an outdated runtime lacking modern security features (script block logging, constrained language mode, AMSI reliability).
controls.rationale: PowerShell 2.0 is deprecated and can be used to bypass modern PowerShell logging/telemetry. Remove or disable where possible and validate that no required legacy software depends on it.

os_applicability.notes: PowerShell v2 is an optional Windows Feature on modern clients; can be disabled via Windows Optional Features / DISM / PowerShell cmdlets.

validation_checks.check: powershell.exe -Version 2 -Command "'v2'" should return error or launch default engine (i.e. not start v2 engine).
HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\PowerShell\1\PowerShellEngine\PowerShellVersion should not report '2.0' as the effective engine in use for systems where v2 feature removed.
validation_checks.note: Registry keys are informative; presence of '2.0' in older systems may require additional checks. Use optional feature state as authoritative on modern OS.

failure_modes: Some legacy installers or scheduled tasks that explicitly call powershell.exe -Version 2 may fail; test critical apps before mass removal.
Group Policy / configuration management clients might assume v2 host for legacy scripts.

references.note: Registry locations for PowerShell version detection.

os_applicability.notes: Server features can be removed via DISM or Uninstall-WindowsFeature depending on role packaging.

enforcement_methods.verify_after: Get-WindowsFeature -Name PowerShell-V2 → InstallState should be 'Removed' or 'Available'.

failure_modes: Legacy server automation depending on v2 may break; check orchestration tools and installers.

os_applicability.notes: PowerShell v2 is often baked into Windows 7 images. To move away from v2 you must upgrade PowerShell (WMF) to a newer WMF (e.g. 3.0 or 5.1) where v2 behavior is superseded; removal as an optional feature is not always possible on vanilla Win7 installs. Test thoroughly.

enforcement_methods.command: Install appropriate Windows Management Framework (WMF) package (WMF 3.0 / WMF 4.0 / WMF 5.1) per Microsoft prerequisites (SP1, .NET). See official WMF package.
enforcement_methods.note: After a supported WMF upgrade, evaluate optional feature state and vendor guidance before attempting removal.

validation_checks.check: Attempt Get-WindowsOptionalFeature / DISM queries; if feature not present as removable, mark as 'unsupported' and use mitigation by migration.

failure_modes: On Windows 7, removing/upgrading PowerShell without following WMF prerequisites or testing will likely break legacy applications and installers. Do not auto-apply at scale.

references.note: Practical note: Win7 often has v2 baked-in; removal not identical to modern Windows.

controls.implementation_notes: Inventory scripts and scheduled tasks for explicit '-Version 2' invocations and vendor installers that require v2. For systems where v2 cannot be removed, document as 'not removable' + plan migration or vendor updates.

controls.name: PowerShell Constrained Language Mode
controls.description: Ensure PowerShell runs in Constrained Language mode on hosts where a system-wide application-control policy (WDAC/Device Guard / AppLocker UMCI) is enforced. Constrained Language reduces attack surface by blocking risky language features and API access.
controls.rationale: Constrained Language mode greatly reduces the ability of malicious scripts to call arbitrary Win32/.NET APIs; it is intended to be used together with system-wide application control enforcement (UMCI/WDAC/AppLocker).

os_applicability.notes: Constrained Language is effective when UMCI/WDAC or AppLocker script enforcement is active. PowerShell 5.1 or PowerShell 7+ required for policy detection behavior described in Microsoft guidance.
No reliable system-wide enforcement available on legacy platforms; test carefully and prefer migration to newer OS where UMCI/WDAC is supported.

enforcement_methods.type: Recommended — Application control (WDAC / Device Guard / AppLocker UMCI)
enforcement_methods.description: Deploy and enforce a UMCI/WDAC or AppLocker policy that results in user-mode code integrity (UMCI) enforcement; PowerShell will then automatically run in Constrained Language mode for untrusted contexts.
enforcement_methods.implementation_notes: Create WDAC/UMCI policy using ConfigCI tooling, sign and deploy as Enforced. Add trusted signed scripts/modules to the policy to allow full-language where needed.

enforcement_methods.type: Fallback / lab/test (environment variable — NOT a secure enforcement)
enforcement_methods.description: Set the __PSLockdownPolicy environment value to '4' to force ConstrainedLanguage for testing; this is not a secure enforcement in production because it can be changed by local attackers.
enforcement_methods.notes: Microsoft documents the environment variable as a test hook and explicitly warns it is not a replacement for UMCI/WDAC enforcement.

validation_checks.success_criteria: Returns 'ConstrainedLanguage' in an interactive session or in a test run started under the target enforcement context.

validation_checks.type: Verify UMCI/WDAC enforcement (authoritative trigger for system-wide mode)
validation_checks.success_criteria: UsermodeCodeIntegrityPolicyEnforcementStatus / CodeIntegrityPolicyEnforcementStatus indicates '2' (Enforced) or equivalent enforcement flag per environment.

validation_checks.command: [System.Console]::WriteLine('x')  (or try to call a blocked API such as COM/ Add-Type)
validation_checks.success_criteria: Blocked operations should fail with 'MethodInvocationNotSupportedInConstrainedLanguage' or similar; approved signed modules included in policy may run in FullLanguage.

audit_query_examples: Check presence of active WDAC policy files: C:\Windows\System32\CodeIntegrity\CiPolicies\Active\*.cip
failure_modes: If enforcement is applied without adding required administrator-signed automation/scripts to the policy, legitimate management scripts will break.
Environment-variable enforcement is trivially bypassable by local attackers and must not be relied on in production.
Constrained Language may disable scheduled job modules and other automation that relies on .NET serialization.
controls.privilege_required: Administrator for WDAC/UMCI policy deployment; machine-level registry changes require Administrator / SYSTEM.
controls.functional_impact: High — may break advanced automation and unsigned management scripts if policy not tuned.

priority.suggested_priority: High for high-value hosts; Medium for general endpoints
priority.priority_reason: Provides strong mitigation when paired with application control; high operational impact if misapplied.

references.title: PowerShell Constrained Language Mode — PowerShell Team (Microsoft DevBlogs)
Set PowerShell language mode to Constrained — Microsoft Q&A (notes: __PSLockdownPolicy)
Verify Device Guard / UMCI via Win32_DeviceGuard (examples and enforcement status)

controls.implementation_notes: Preferred implementation path: design WDAC/UMCI policy scoped to host-class, include trusted signed management scripts/modules to avoid operational breakage, deploy in Audit then Enforce. Use environment variable only for controlled testing.

controls.name: Disable Windows Script Host (WSH)
controls.description: Prevent execution of VBScript and JScript via Windows Script Host by disabling WSH where it is not required.
controls.rationale: WSH is frequently abused by phishing/malware to run script payloads; disabling it reduces an important attack vector for script-based persistence and execution.

os_applicability.notes: WSH can be disabled per-user or machine-wide via registry keys; on modern Windows additional capability removal (VBSCRIPT capability) may also be available.

enforcement_methods.description: Create or set the Disabled value under the Windows Script Host Settings key to block script host.
commands: reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Script Host\Settings" /v Enabled /t REG_DWORD /d 0 /f
reg add "HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Script Host\Settings" /v Enabled /t REG_DWORD /d 0 /f  (to block only current user)
enforcement_methods.notes: Setting 'Enabled' to 0 disables WSH. Microsoft docs show this as supported method to disable WSH.

enforcement_methods.description: On some modern images, VBScript can be removed via Windows capability management (Remove-WindowsCapability / DISM / Remove-WindowsPackage) depending on OS packaging.
enforcement_methods.notes: Capability names and availability vary by OS; validate on target images before mass removal.

validation_checks.command: Attempt to run a simple test script (e.g., echo 'hello' in test.vbs) using cscript.exe or wscript.exe
validation_checks.success_criteria: Execution blocked with message 'Windows Script Host access is disabled on this machine.' or script fails to run.
VBSCRIPT capability absent or removed (if capability-removal approach used).

failure_modes: Legacy installers, backup agents, or management tooling that rely on WSH may fail — validate vendor software before removal.
Per-user key may lead to inconsistent behavior across accounts; prefer machine-wide policy for enterprise consistency.
controls.privilege_required: Administrator for machine-wide registry or capability removal.
controls.functional_impact: Medium — will break legitimate scripts that rely on WSH.

priority.priority_reason: Reduces a common attack vector with manageable compatibility risk if tested.

references.title: How to disable Windows Script Host (MS Q&A / Microsoft guidance showing registry entries)
F-Secure guidance: disable Windows Script Host

controls.implementation_notes: Inventory scheduled tasks, startup items, backup scripts and vendor installers; perform staged pilot (10–50 devices) and monitor for failures; consider per-user vs machine-wide depending on management model.

controls.name: Disable Office macros / Enforce restrictive macro policy
controls.description: Configure Microsoft Office macro settings to disable macros or require explicit administrator approval/digitally-signed macros.
controls.rationale: Office macros are a primary phishing/malware vector. Enforcing 'Disable all macros except digitally signed' or 'Disable all without notification' at policy level reduces macro-based compromise risk.

os_applicability.notes: Registry/GPO paths differ across Office versions (12.0/14.0/15.0/16.0). Use the Policies path (HKLM/HKCU\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Office\<ver>\<app>\Security) for enforceable settings.

enforcement_methods.description: Use Administrative Templates (Office ADMX) for 'VBA Macro Notification Settings' and set to 'Disable all except digitally signed macros' or 'Disable all without notification' depending on risk appetite.
enforcement_methods.gpo_path: User Configuration → Administrative Templates → Microsoft <App> <year> → <App> Options → Security → Trust Center → VBA Macro Notification Settings

enforcement_methods.type: Registry (Policies hive) — for unmanaged or script-based deployment
enforcement_methods.notes: VBAWarnings values: 1 = Enable all macros; 2 = Disable all with notification; 3 = Disable all except digitally signed macros; 4 = Disable all without notification. Use the Policies path to prevent user change.

enforcement_methods.description: Use Intune configuration profiles or Office CSPs to apply macro restrictions for cloud-managed devices; verify behavior on Click-to-Run installations.
enforcement_methods.notes: Some CSP/Click-to-Run combinations need testing; central management is recommended for enterprise scale.

validation_checks.success_criteria: Value exists and equals approved policy (e.g., 3 or 4).

validation_checks.command: Open a test Office document with a simple unsigned macro and verify macro is blocked/not executed or prompts per policy.
validation_checks.success_criteria: Macro execution blocked or only allowed if digitally signed and trusted.

validation_checks.command: Compare registry values against benchmark (CIS/STIG) guidance for Office macro settings.

audit_query_examples: Test opening document with unsigned macro and observe behavior
Check applied GPO: gpresult /h gpresults.html and review Office administrative template settings
failure_modes: Blocking macros may disrupt business processes that rely on signed macros — require vendor/line-of-business exception process for signed macros.
Click-to-Run Office versions may not honor some legacy registry paths; validate for your Office deployment type.
controls.privilege_required: Administrator (for HKLM/Policies registry or Intune deployment), or delegated GPO rights.
controls.functional_impact: Medium — will prevent many legitimate macros; allow a signed-macro exception process where needed.

priority.priority_reason: Macro exploitation is a high-frequency initial access vector; policy blocking is effective and widely recommended.

references.title: Enable or disable macros in Microsoft 365 files — Microsoft Support
Office macro registry/GPO references (VBAWarnings mapping / STIG examples)
STIG / benchmark examples showing registry key values for VBAWarnings (illustrative enforcement)

controls.implementation_notes: Use Policies registry hive (HKCU/HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Office\<ver>) to prevent user override. Build an exception workflow for vendor-signed macros (whitelist by publisher cert) and test Click-to-Run packaging behavior in pilot.
